title: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight
authors: Alexander G. Marneros, David R. Bickers

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.title: Understanding UV Spectrum's Role in Photosensitivity
items.content: The sun emits energy across the ultraviolet (UV), visible, and infrared spectra. The UV spectrum is divided into UV-B (290-320 nm) and UV-A (320-400 nm). UV-B is significantly more efficient at causing skin erythema compared to UV-A, which is about 1000 times less effective.
keyPoints: UV-B wavelengths range from 290 to 320 nm and are known as the 'sunburn spectrum'.
UV-A wavelengths range from 320 to 400 nm and are ~1000-fold less efficient in producing skin erythema than UV-B.
Photosensitivity requires a photosensitizing chemical that absorbs energy and transfers it, causing reactions.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.title: Factors Affecting Solar Radiation Exposure on Earth
items.content: Solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface varies due to several factors including seasonal changes, ozone layer thickness, altitude, latitude, and atmospheric conditions such as cloud cover and pollution. These variations influence the amount of UV energy that reaches the skin.
keyPoints: There is a 20-fold regional variation in solar flux at 300 nm reaching Earth's surface.
Altitude increases solar exposure by approximately 4% for every 300 meters.
Solar intensity increases with decreasing latitude.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: Penetration Depth of UV Radiation in Skin
items.content: Understanding how different wavelengths of UV radiation penetrate the skin is crucial for assessing potential damage and therapeutic interventions. UV-B is primarily absorbed by the stratum corneum, with less than 10% reaching the dermis. In contrast, UV-A penetrates more deeply into the dermis, contributing to photoaging.
keyPoints: Less than 10% of incident solar UV-B wavelengths penetrate from the epidermis to the dermis.
Approximately 3% of radiation below 300 nm, 20% below 360 nm, and 33% of short visible radiation reach the basal cell layer in untanned skin.
UV-A can alter structural proteins in the dermis, leading to photoaging.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: DNA Damage and Repair Mechanisms in UV-Induced Skin Effects
items.content: UV-B radiation induces structural changes in epidermal DNA, particularly between pyrimidine bases, leading to potentially mutagenic effects. Efficient repair mechanisms are vital for preventing skin cancers such as BCC, SCC, and MCC.
keyPoints: UV-B causes formation of cyclobutane dimers and 6,4-photoproducts in DNA.
Defective DNA repair increases the risk of cutaneous cancer.
Xeroderma pigmentosum patients show increased skin cancer frequency due to deficient UV-induced photoproduct repair.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.title: Role of Chromophores in Skin Damage and Photodynamic Therapy
items.content: Chromophores, including endogenous components like nucleic acids and exogenous ones like porphyrins, absorb solar energy. In diseases like porphyrias, increased porphyrin levels lead to ROS generation and skin damage. Conversely, photodynamic therapy (PDT) uses photoexcited porphyrins to selectively target tumor cells for destruction.
keyPoints: Chromophores absorb incident solar energy, leading to potential skin damage.
Increased porphyrins in porphyrias cause ROS-mediated structural skin damage.
PDT utilizes porphyrin precursors like 5-aminolevulinic acid for targeted tumor cell destruction.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.title: Mechanism and Risk Factors for Sunburn
items.content: Sunburn is an inflammatory response primarily triggered by UV-B radiation, with its severity influenced by melanin pigmentation levels in the skin. Individuals with lower melanin (e.g., red hair/fair skin) have a higher risk of sunburn due to less effective photoprotection.
keyPoints: Sunburn is an acute inflammatory response predominantly to UV-B radiation.
Melanin provides photoprotection; individuals with low MC1R activity (e.g., red hair, fair skin) have a higher risk of sunburn and melanoma due to lower eumelanin levels.
Pheomelanin has weaker shielding capacity against UVR compared to eumelanin.
Fitzpatrick classification categorizes skin types from I (always burn, never tan) to VI (never burn, always tan).
Sunburn erythema involves vasodilation and cytokine release; UV-B is more efficient than UV-A in causing erythema.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Factors Influencing Skin Response to UVR
items.content: The melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R) gene plays a crucial role in determining skin pigmentation and response to UV radiation, with mutations affecting susceptibility to sunburn and melanoma.
keyPoints: MC1R is central to the tanning response; mutations can lead to variations in skin color and UVR sensitivity.
Individuals with red hair/fair skin typically have low MC1R activity, increasing their risk of melanoma due to higher pheomelanin levels.
Pheomelanin may promote melanoma formation through oxidative damage and UV-A-induced ROS.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.title: Pathophysiology of Sunburn Erythema
items.content: Sunburn erythema results from vasodilation and the release of cytokines, growth factors, and ROS following UVR exposure, with UV-B being more efficient than UV-A in inducing this response.
keyPoints: Sunburn erythema involves vasodilation of dermal blood vessels with a lag time of 4–12 hours post-exposure.
UV-B is more efficient than UV-A in causing sunburn erythema, though UV-A contributes at midday when its levels are higher.
Cytokines and vasoactive mediators released during UVR exposure attract inflammatory cells to the site.
Eicosanoids like prostaglandin E2 play a role in inducing sunburn erythema; NSAIDs can reduce this effect.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.title: Vitamin D Synthesis via UV-B Exposure
items.content: UV-B radiation initiates the conversion of epidermal 7-dehydrocholesterol to pre-vitamin D3, which is then transformed into vitamin D3. This process occurs in the skin and involves subsequent conversion in the liver and kidneys to form the active hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3.
keyPoints: UV-B causes photolysis of epidermal 7-dehydrocholesterol to pre-vitamin D3.
Pre-vitamin D3 undergoes isomerization to vitamin D3.
Vitamin D3 circulates and converts in the liver and kidney to 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3.
Topical synthetic vitamin D analogues are used therapeutically for psoriasis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.title: Controversy on Sunscreen Use and Vitamin D Levels
items.content: There is no definitive evidence that sunscreen use significantly reduces vitamin D levels. Despite concerns about UV-B filtering leading to potential vitamin D deficiency, especially in the elderly, nutritional supplementation is recommended for those deficient.
keyPoints: No clear-cut evidence suggests sunscreens substantially diminish vitamin D levels.
Elderly may be at risk of vitamin D deficiency due to sunscreen use and reduced skin capacity to produce vitamin D3.
Nutritional supplementation is a preferable strategy for addressing vitamin D deficiency.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.subtopic: Chronic Effects of Sun Exposure: Malignant
items.title: Skin Cancer Induction by UV-B
items.content: UV-B exposure initiates skin cancer through mutagenic changes in DNA, leading to the development of non-melanoma skin cancers (NMSC) such as SCCs and BCCs. This process involves initiation, promotion, and progression stages.
keyPoints: UV-B acts as both a tumor initiator and promoter in skin cancer induction.
Initiation involves irreversible DNA changes in keratinocytes.
Promotion leads to clonal expansion of initiated cells forming actinic keratoses, potentially progressing to SCCs.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.title: Role of UV-induced Mutations in Skin Carcinogenesis
items.content: Exposure to ultraviolet radiation (UVR) leads to the accumulation of gene mutations that drive skin carcinogenesis, particularly in squamous cell carcinoma (SCC). These include mutations in genes like NOTCH1 and p53. The mutation patterns in aged sun-exposed skin overlap significantly with those found in SCCs.
keyPoints: UV-induced oncogenic driver mutations are detectable in both normal aged sun-exposed skin and SCCs.
~20% of normal aged sun-exposed skin cells and ~60% of SCCs carry driver mutations in NOTCH1.
SCCs harbor ~10 times more oncogenic driver mutations per cell than cells in aged sun-exposed normal skin.
Characteristic UVR-induced p53 mutations include C → T and CC → TT transitions.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.title: Distinct Mutational Profiles in Basal Cell Carcinoma
items.content: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) has a unique mutational profile compared to SCC, primarily involving the patched gene or smoothened oncogene, leading to activation of the sonic hedgehog signaling pathway. This distinction is crucial for understanding the molecular pathogenesis and treatment strategies.
keyPoints: BCCs harbor inactivating mutations particularly in the tumor-suppressor gene patched.
Activating mutations in the oncogene smoothened result in constitutive activation of the sonic hedgehog signaling pathway.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.title: Merkel Cell Carcinoma Pathogenesis
items.content: Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) can be either Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV) positive or negative, with distinct pathogenetic mechanisms. MCPyV-positive MCCs involve viral integration and oncoprotein production, while MCPyV-negative ones show UV-induced mutations.
keyPoints: MCC incidence is about 1 in 100,000 globally.
Overall survival rates: ~50% for local disease, 35% for nodal disease, 15% for metastatic disease.
MCPyV-positive MCCs involve viral integration and truncating mutations of the large T antigen.
MCPyV-negative MCCs show high levels of UV-induced signature mutations (C to T or CC to TT).
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.title: Sunburn and Vitamin D Synthesis: Balancing Benefits and Risks
items.content: Exposure to sunlight results in acute effects such as sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. While necessary for health, excessive exposure increases the risk of non-melanoma skin cancers (NMSCs) like basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC).
keyPoints: Approximately 80% of NMSCs develop on sun-exposed areas such as face, neck, and hands.
Major risk factors include male sex, childhood sun exposure, older age, fair skin, and residence near the equator.
Lifetime risk for a fair-skinned individual to develop an NMSC is estimated at ~15%.
The incidence of NMSC is increasing by 2–3% per year.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)

items.title: Melanoma and Sunlight: A Complex Relationship
items.content: While the link between sun exposure and melanoma is less direct than with NMSCs, strong associations exist. Melanomas can arise from early childhood sun exposure and are influenced by factors such as family history and dysplastic nevi.
keyPoints: Risk of melanoma does not correlate directly with cumulative sun exposure but may relate to the duration and extent of exposure in childhood.
Melanomas often develop on non-sun-exposed skin, suggesting UVR-independent risk factors.
BRAF and NRAS mutations are common in melanoma, leading to targeted therapies like immune checkpoint inhibitors.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)

items.title: Photoimmunology: The Impact of Solar Radiation on Immune Function
items.content: Solar radiation causes both local and systemic immunosuppression, affecting the innate and adaptive immune systems. This can lead to decreased immune responses at irradiated sites (local) and remote sites (systemic).
keyPoints: Local immunosuppression involves depletion of Langerhans cells in the epidermis after UV-B exposure.
Systemic effects include diminished immune response to antigens introduced at non-irradiated sites.
High doses of UVR can lead to systemic immunosuppressive effects.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.title: Mechanisms of UV-Induced Immunosuppression
items.content: UV radiation induces immunosuppression through various mechanisms, including the formation of cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers in Langerhans cells, which inhibit antigen presentation. The trans-cis isomerization of urocanic acid also contributes to this effect.
keyPoints: Major chromophores initiating UV-mediated immunosuppression include DNA and trans-urocanic acid.
UV-induced cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers in Langerhans cells inhibit antigen presentation.
Trans-cis isomerization of urocanic acid drives immunosuppression.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.title: UV-Induced Systemic Immunosuppression
items.content: UVR-induced systemic immunosuppression involves various cytokines such as IL-4, IL-10, and eicosanoids. This leads to the induction of regulatory T cells and natural killer T cells, contributing to both cell-mediated and humoral immunosuppression.
keyPoints: Cytokines implicated include tumor necrosis factor-$ \\$alpha $, IL-4, IL-10, and eicosanoids.
Induction of regulatory T cells and natural killer T cells linked to UVR-induced immunosuppression.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.title: Impact of Chronic UV Exposure on Skin Cancer Risk
items.content: Chronic UV exposure enhances the risk of non-melanoma skin cancers (NMSC) by activating regulatory T cells that suppress antitumor immunity. Organ transplant recipients, who are immunosuppressed, have a significantly higher incidence of NMSC.
keyPoints: UV-B activates regulatory T cells suppressing antitumor responses via IL-10.
Over 50% of organ transplant recipients develop BCCs and SCCs.
Nonwhite transplant recipients have lower rates of skin cancers compared to white recipients.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.title: Diagnosis and Clinical Presentation of Polymorphous Light Eruption (PMLE)
items.content: PMLE is the most common photosensitivity disease, often presenting with pruritic erythematous papules on exposed skin areas. It typically manifests in spring due to initial sun exposure but may subside with continued exposure ('hardening'). Diagnosis can be supported by photostep procedures and a biopsy.
keyPoints: PMLE is more common in women than men.
Juvenile spring eruption, a variant of PMLE, presents as erythematous scaly papules or bullae on ears, face, or dorsal hands.
Pruritic skin lesions may occur within hours of sun exposure and persist for weeks without scarring.
Action spectrum usually involves UV-A and UV-B.
Treatment includes topical/systemic glucocorticoids; prevention involves high SPF sunscreen and prophylactic phototherapy.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Actinic Prurigo and Its Management
items.content: Actinic prurigo is a photo-induced pruritic eruption similar to PMLE but can persist throughout the summer and into winter. It often occurs in individuals with a familial predisposition, and photoprotective measures are crucial.
keyPoints: Actinic prurigo shares similarities with PMLE but has a longer duration.
A familial predisposition is common.
Photoprotective measures include high SPF sunscreen.
Treatment options are similar to PMLE: corticosteroids or prophylactic phototherapy.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Hydroa Vacciniforme
items.content: Hydroa vacciniforme is a childhood photodermatosis characterized by vesicular lesions on sun-exposed skin, often healing with depressed scars. It may be linked to latent Epstein-Barr virus infection.
keyPoints: Manifests in children during summer with vesicular lesions on the face and dorsal hands.
Lesions heal with depressed scars.
A suggested link exists between hydroa vacciniforme and latent EBV infection.
Photoprotective measures are essential to prevent manifestation.
Treatment includes photodesensitization with NB UVB, antimalarials, or intermittent systemic corticosteroids.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Phototoxicity from Photoallergy
items.content: Phototoxic reactions are nonimmunologic and resemble sunburn, while photoallergic reactions are immunopathologic, presenting as pruritic eczematous dermatitis. UV-A is more commonly involved in both.
keyPoints: Phototoxicity manifests with erythema resembling a sunburn that desquamates within days; edema, vesicles, and bullae may occur.
Photoallergy typically presents as intensely pruritic eczematous dermatitis evolving into lichenified changes in sun-exposed areas.
UV-A exposure is more commonly associated with both phototoxicity and photoallergy.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis of Phototoxicity and Photoallergy
items.content: Photostep procedures, including determining the minimal erythemal dose (MED) and photopatch testing, are used to diagnose phototoxicity and photoallergy.
keyPoints: Determining MED with suspected agents helps identify causative drugs in phototoxicity.
Photopatch testing confirms photoallergy by applying known photoallergens and irradiating one set with UV-A.
A positive result in photopatch testing is the development of eczematous changes at sites exposed to sensitizer and light.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Management Strategies for Persistent Light Reaction and Chronic Actinic Dermatitis
items.content: Persistent light reaction and chronic actinic dermatitis require strict photoprotection, avoidance of known allergens, and potentially systemic immunosuppression.
keyPoints: Strict photoprotection and avoidance of contact/photocontact allergens are crucial for managing persistent light reactions and chronic actinic dermatitis.
Patients with chronic actinic dermatitis often require high-SPF (>30) sunscreens and may need systemic immunosuppression like azathioprine in severe cases.
A small subset (5–10%) of patients with photoallergy may develop persistent light reaction even after eliminating the offending agent.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Porphyria Cutanea Tarda (PCT) and its Pathophysiology
items.content: Porphyria cutanea tarda is the most prevalent type of porphyria, characterized by decreased activity of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase (UROD). This enzyme deficiency leads to increased hepatic iron stores and photosensitivity. PCT can be sporadic or inherited, with both types showing reduced UROD activity.
keyPoints: PCT is associated with decreased UROD activity to <20% of normal.
Increased hepatic iron stores are a feature in both sporadic and inherited forms.
Chronic photosensitivity manifests as vesicles and bullae on sun-exposed skin, often healing slowly with crusting and purplish discoloration.
Diagnosis confirmed by urinary porphyrin excretion, plasma porphyrin assay, and UROD activity assays.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Porphyria Cutanea Tarda
items.content: The management of PCT involves reducing hepatic iron stores and avoiding porphyrinogenic agents. Phlebotomies are used to decrease iron levels, while hydroxychloroquine is administered orally in low doses twice weekly.
keyPoints: Treatment includes repeated phlebotomies to reduce hepatic iron stores.
Oral administration of low-dose hydroxychloroquine (twice weekly) is effective for PCT.
Avoidance of ethanol, estrogens, and sun exposure is crucial in management.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Distinguishing Pseudoporphyria from Porphyria Cutanea Tarda
items.content: Pseudoporphyria resembles PCT clinically but lacks abnormalities in porphyrin metabolism. It is associated with chronic kidney disease, hemodialysis, tanning bed use, or certain medications like naproxen.
keyPoints: Pseudoporphyria does not have abnormal porphyrin metabolism.
Associated factors include chronic kidney disease, hemodialysis, and certain medications.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.title: Management of Erythropoietic Protoporphyria (EPP)
items.content: Erythropoietic protoporphyria is managed by rigorous sunlight protection due to its painful photosensitivity. Afamelanotide, a synthetic peptide analogue of $ \alpha $-MSH approved by the FDA, increases skin pigmentation through melanogenesis and improves sun exposure tolerance in EPP patients.
keyPoints: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is caused by decreased activity of ferrochelatase due to genetic mutations.
Major clinical features include painful burning and stinging of exposed skin upon sun exposure, with potential for waxlike scarring after repeated episodes.
Afamelanotide approved by FDA increases melanogenesis, improving tolerance to sunlight in EPP patients.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.title: Effective Photoprotection Strategies
items.content: Photoprotection strategies include natural protection by melanin, use of clothing and sunscreens. Sunscreens rated by SPF protect against UV-B but not necessarily UV-A; broad-spectrum sunscreens with SPF $ \geq $30 and water resistance are recommended for adequate protection.
keyPoints: Natural photoprotection is provided by keratins and melanin in the epidermis.
Clothing reduces direct exposure, while sunscreens rated on an SPF scale offer varying levels of UV-B protection.
Broad-spectrum sunscreens with SPF $ \geq $30, broad-spectrum coverage, and water resistance are recommended.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.title: Sunscreen Ingredients and Their Functions
items.content: Sunscreen ingredients vary in their UV protection capabilities. Cinnamates, PABA derivatives, and salicylates absorb UV-B; benzophenones and avobenzone protect against UV-A1 and UV-A2. Physical blockers like zinc oxide and titanium dioxide offer broad-spectrum protection.
keyPoints: Cinnamates, PABA derivatives, and salicylates are effective in absorbing UV-B radiation.
Benzophenones and avobenzone protect against UV-A1 and UV-A2 respectively.
Zinc oxide and titanium dioxide provide broad-spectrum protection by absorbing or reflecting UVR.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.title: NB-UV-B vs. PUVA for Psoriasis: Efficacy and Safety
items.content: NB-UV-B phototherapy, using fluorescent bulbs emitting radiation at approximately 311 nm, is more efficacious than broadband UV-B in treating psoriasis. It also poses a lower risk of skin cancer compared to PUVA photochemotherapy.
keyPoints: NB-UV-B emits radiation at ~311 nm
Enhanced efficacy over broadband UV-B for psoriasis
Lower risk of skin cancer than PUVA
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOTHERAPY AND PHOTOCHEMOTHERAPY

items.title: PUVA Photochemotherapy Mechanism and Applications
items.content: PUVA involves the use of psoralens combined with UV-A to treat conditions like psoriasis, early-stage cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, and vitiligo. Psoralens intercalate into DNA, forming adducts that decrease DNA synthesis, contributing to therapeutic effects.
keyPoints: Psoralens form adducts with pyrimidine bases in DNA
Effective for psoriasis, early-stage cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, and vitiligo
Induces apoptosis of atypical T lymphocytes
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOTHERAPY AND PHOTOCHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.question: Which component of solar radiation is primarily responsible for preventing the penetration of shorter, more energetic wavelengths to the Earth's surface?
items.explanation: Stratospheric ozone prevents the penetration of shorter, more energetic UV wavelengths.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.question: What is the major cause of human skin cancer related to sunlight exposure?
items.explanation: Cutaneous exposure to UV-B radiation, a component of solar radiation, is a major cause of human skin cancer.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.question: Which wavelength range in the photobiologic action spectrum is most efficient at producing erythema in human skin?
items.explanation: The UV-B range (290-320 nm) is the most efficient in producing erythema, known as the 'sunburn spectrum'.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.question: Which layer of the epidermis is a major absorber of UV-B radiation?
items.explanation: The stratum corneum is the outermost layer of the epidermis and a major absorber of UV-B radiation.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.question: What percentage of incident solar UV-B wavelengths penetrate from the epidermis to the dermis?
items.explanation: Less than 10% of incident solar UV-B wavelengths penetrate from the epidermis to the dermis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.question: Which type of skin cancer is associated with defective DNA repair mechanisms?
items.explanation: Individuals with defective DNA repair mechanisms are at high risk for nonmelanoma skin cancers, including basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.question: What is the role of 7-dehydrocholesterol in the skin?
options: It acts as a chromophore for UV-B absorption.
It serves as a precursor to vitamin D.
It protects against DNA damage by absorbing UVA radiation.
items.explanation: 7-dehydrocholesterol is an endogenous chromophore that absorbs UV-B radiation and serves as a precursor to vitamin D.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.question: Which type of melanin provides weaker shielding capacity against UVR?
items.explanation: Pheomelanin has a much weaker shielding capacity against UVR compared to eumelanin.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of $\alpha$-melanocyte-stimulating hormone ($\alpha$-MSH) in response to UVR?
options: It stimulates increased transcription of the microphthalmia-associated transcription factor (MITF)
items.explanation: $\alpha$-MSH leads to increased intracellular cAMP and protein kinase A activation, resulting in increased transcription of MITF, which stimulates melanogenesis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.question: Which gene mutation contributes to variations in human skin and hair color?
items.explanation: Mutations in the melanocortin-1 receptor (MC1R) gene contribute to genetic variations in human skin and hair color.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary cause of sunburn erythema?
items.explanation: Sunburn erythema is due to vasodilation of dermal blood vessels.

items.question: Which compound undergoes temperature-dependent isomerization to form vitamin D3 after photolysis by UV-B?
items.explanation: UV-B causes photolysis of epidermal 7-dehydrocholesterol, converting it to pre-vitamin D3.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.question: Where is vitamin D3 converted into the functional hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3?
items.explanation: Vitamin D3 diffuses to the dermal vasculature and is converted in the liver and kidney.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.question: What therapeutic use is mentioned for synthetic vitamin D analogues?
options: Inhibition of keratinocyte proliferation in psoriasis
items.explanation: Synthetic vitamin D analogues are used therapeutically to inhibit keratinocyte proliferation in psoriasis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.question: What is the current consensus on sunscreen use and its impact on vitamin D levels?
options: Sunscreens substantially diminish vitamin D levels.
No clear-cut evidence suggests sunscreens diminish vitamin D levels.
Sunscreens have no effect on skin health.
items.explanation: There is no clear-cut evidence that the use of sunscreens substantially diminishes vitamin D levels.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is an acute effect of skin exposure to sunlight?
items.explanation: The passage states that sunburn and vitamin D synthesis are acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.question: What is a molecular consequence of UV-induced mutations in aged sun-exposed normal skin?
options: Activation of the sonic hedgehog pathway
items.explanation: UV-induced oncogenic driver mutations in aged sun-exposed normal skin lead to a growth advantage and precancerous clones.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.question: Which gene mutation is commonly found in both aged sun-exposed normal skin and SCCs?
items.explanation: ~20% of normal aged sun-exposed skin cells and ~60% of SCCs carry driver mutations in NOTCH1.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.question: What type of mutation is characteristic of UVR-induced p53 mutations?
items.explanation: UVR-induced p53 mutations are characterized by C → T and CC → TT transitions.

items.question: What is one chronic effect of prolonged sun exposure?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that NMSCs are linked to excessive sun exposure, indicating a chronic effect.

items.question: Which factor does NOT increase the risk of NMSCs?
items.explanation: The passage notes that individuals with darker-pigmented skin have a lower risk of skin cancer.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)

items.question: Which type of melanoma is associated with BRAF mutations?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that BRAF and NRAS mutations are frequently found in melanoma.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.question: Which chromophores in the upper epidermis are known to initiate UV-mediated immunosuppression?
options: DNA, trans-urocanic acid, and membrane components
items.explanation: The passage states that DNA, trans-urocanic acid, and membrane components are chromophores known to initiate UV-mediated immunosuppression.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.question: What is the role of cis-urocanic acid in UVR-induced immunosuppression?
options: It promotes antigen presentation by Langerhans cells.
It inhibits antigen presentation by Langerhans cells.
It has no effect on immune responses.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that cis-urocanic acid may exert its immunosuppressive effects through inhibition of antigen presentation by Langerhans cells.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.question: Which cytokine is associated with UVR-induced systemic immunosuppression?
items.explanation: The passage lists tumor necrosis factor-α as one of the cytokines implicated in UVR-induced systemic immunosuppression.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.question: What is a consequence of chronic sun exposure related to the immune system?
items.explanation: The passage mentions that chronic sun exposure and associated immunosuppression enhance the risk of skin cancer.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is characterized by vesicular lesions that heal with depressed scars on sun-exposed skin, particularly in children?
items.explanation: Hydroa vacciniforme is a photodermatosis of childhood that manifests with vesicular lesions on sun-exposed skin, often healing with depressed scars.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.question: Which photosensitivity disorder typically begins in infancy or early childhood and is associated with erythropoietic protoporphyria?
items.explanation: The acute photosensitivity of erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) almost always begins in infancy or early childhood.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical age of onset for the chronic photosensitivity associated with porphyria cutanea tarda?
items.explanation: The chronic photosensitivity of porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) typically begins in the fourth and fifth decades of life.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.question: Which photoprotective measure is NOT mentioned as a preventive approach for Polymorphous Light Eruption?
items.explanation: Topical antifungal creams are not mentioned as a preventive approach for Polymorphous Light Eruption.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.question: Which type of photosensitivity is characterized by an intensely pruritic eczematous dermatitis that evolves into lichenified, thickened changes in sun-exposed areas?
items.explanation: Photoallergy is characterized by intensely pruritic eczematous dermatitis that evolves into lichenified, thickened changes.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.question: What type of reaction occurs within minutes of sun exposure and usually resolves within 24 hours?
items.explanation: Solar urticaria is an immediate histamine-mediated immunologic reaction to sunlight that resolves within 24 hours.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.question: Which condition involves a persistent hypersensitivity to light even after the offending agent is eliminated?
items.explanation: A small subset of patients with photoallergy may develop a persistent light reaction.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.question: Which type of photosensitivity is often confirmed diagnostically by photostep procedures?
items.explanation: Phototoxicity can be confirmed by determining the minimal erythemal dose (MED) with and without exposure to a suspected agent.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.question: Which enzyme deficiency is associated with Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT)?
items.explanation: PCT is associated with decreased activity of the enzyme uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.question: Which type of porphyria is characterized by acute nonblistering photosensitivity?
items.explanation: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is an acute, cutaneous, nonblistering porphyria.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.question: What is a common treatment for Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT)?
items.explanation: Treatment for PCT includes repeated phlebotomies to reduce hepatic iron stores and intermittent low doses of hydroxychloroquine.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.question: Which condition is associated with photodermatosis resembling PCT but without porphyrin metabolism abnormalities?
items.explanation: Pseudoporphyria resembles PCT clinically but does not involve abnormalities in porphyrin metabolism.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.question: Which enzyme's decreased activity due to genetic mutations is associated with Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP)?
items.explanation: Erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP) is due to genetic mutations that decrease the activity of ferrochelatase.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.question: What is the primary benefit of afamelanotide in patients with EPP?
options: It increases tolerance to sunlight without pain.
It acts as a physical UV blocker.
items.explanation: Afamelanotide increases skin pigmentation through melanogenesis, allowing patients with EPP to tolerate sun exposure without pain for longer periods.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.question: Which sunscreen ingredient primarily protects against UV-A1?
items.explanation: Avobenzone is the sunscreen ingredient that mainly protects against UV-A1.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.question: What does SPF stand for in the context of sunscreens?
items.explanation: SPF stands for Sun Protection Factor, which is a ratio of the time required to produce sunburn erythema with and without sunscreen application.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.question: Which wavelength range is associated with NB-UV-B treatments for psoriasis?
items.explanation: NB-UV-B treatments emit radiation at approximately 311 nm.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOTHERAPY AND PHOTOCHEMOTHERAPY

items.question: What is the primary mechanism by which PUVA photochemotherapy ameliorates psoriasis?
options: Induces apoptosis of atypical T lymphocytes
Forms DNA cross-links decreasing DNA synthesis
items.explanation: PUVA photochemotherapy forms adducts with pyrimidine bases in DNA, forming cross-links that decrease DNA synthesis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOTHERAPY AND PHOTOCHEMOTHERAPY

items.question: Which phototherapeutic approach is more commonly used for treating vitiligo?
items.explanation: NB-UVB phototherapy is more commonly used for treating vitiligo.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOTHERAPY AND PHOTOCHEMOTHERAPY

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.statement: The UV-A spectrum is more efficient than UV-B at causing skin erythema.
items.explanation: UV-A (320-400 nm) is ~1000-fold less efficient in producing skin erythema compared to UV-B.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION
items.statement: Solar radiation includes components of the ultraviolet, visible, and infrared spectra.
items.explanation: The sun's energy reaching Earth is limited to UV, visible, and portions of the infrared spectrum.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight | SOLAR RADIATION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.statement: UV-A radiation does not penetrate the dermis.
items.explanation: UV-A readily penetrates to the dermis and can alter structural proteins, contributing to photoaging.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.statement: Photodynamic therapy uses UV light to treat skin lesions.
items.explanation: Photodynamic therapy uses visible light, not UV light, and involves photoexcited porphyrins to generate reactive oxygen species for cell death.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.statement: UV-B is more efficient than UV-A in evoking the response for sunburn erythema.
items.explanation: UV-B is much more efficient than UV-A in evoking the response for sunburn erythema.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.statement: Individuals with high MC1R activity have a higher risk of melanoma formation.
items.explanation: Individuals with low MC1R activity, associated with pheomelanin, have an increased risk of melanoma formation.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.statement: UV-B exposure is necessary but not sufficient for the development of skin tumors.
items.explanation: Exposure to UV-B is a necessary step in tumor initiation, but additional factors are required for tumor progression.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.statement: Chronic UVR exposure primarily affects the epidermis.
items.explanation: Chronic UVR exposure primarily targets the dermis and its connective tissue matrix, leading to solar elastosis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.statement: Melanoma development is a direct result of cumulative sun exposure.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the relationship between sun exposure and melanoma is less direct compared to NMSCs.

items.statement: Nonwhite transplant recipients develop BCCs and SCCs more often than white transplant recipients.

items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)

items.statement: Polymorphous Light Eruption typically resolves without scarring.
items.explanation: PMLE resolves without scarring as mentioned in the passage.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.statement: Actinic prurigo is a condition that only occurs during the spring months.
items.explanation: Actinic prurigo often occurs in the spring but can persist throughout the summer and extend into winter.

items.statement: Chronic actinic dermatitis typically affects young individuals.
items.explanation: Chronic actinic dermatitis typically affects elderly patients.

items.statement: Photoallergic reactions are more commonly initiated by UV-B exposure than UV-A.
items.explanation: Most photoallergic reactions are initiated by UV-A rather than UV-B exposure.

items.statement: Porphyria cutanea tarda is associated with increased activity of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase.

items.statement: Erythropoietic protoporphyria results from a deficiency in ferrochelatase.
items.explanation: EPP is caused by a deficiency of the enzyme ferrochelatase.

items.statement: Physical UV blockers such as zinc oxide and titanium dioxide offer broad-spectrum protection against both UV-B and UV-A.

items.statement: The FDA monograph requires sunscreens to be rated on a scale ranging from low (SPF <2) to high (SPF ≥30).
items.explanation: The FDA monograph stipulates that SPF ratings range from minimal (SPF ≥2 and <12), moderate (SPF ≥12 and <30), to high (SPF ≥30, labeled as 30+).
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.statement: PUVA photochemotherapy poses a higher risk of skin cancer development than NB-UV-B.
items.explanation: NB-UV-B is reported to pose a lower risk of skin cancer development compared to PUVA.

items.statement: PUVA photochemotherapy stimulates melanin synthesis.
items.explanation: PUVA stimulates melanin synthesis, which is beneficial in treating vitiligo.

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.assertion: Individuals with xeroderma pigmentosum have a high risk of developing skin cancer.
items.reason: Xeroderma pigmentosum is characterized by deficient repair of UV-induced photoproducts.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the increased risk of skin cancer due to defective DNA repair in xeroderma pigmentosum.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
items.assertion: Reactive oxygen species generated by UVR can lead to photoaging.
items.reason: ROS cause oxidative damage to DNA, lipids, and proteins in the skin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and ROS-induced oxidative damage contributes to photoaging changes like solar elastosis.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · UV RADIATION (UVR) AND SKIN STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.assertion: Individuals with red hair and fair skin typically have low MC1R activity.
items.reason: Low MC1R activity is associated with pheomelanin, which provides weaker shielding capacity against UVR.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)
items.assertion: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) can reduce sunburn erythema.
items.reason: NSAIDs inhibit the release of proinflammatory cytokines and vasoactive mediators.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 1)

items.assertion: Vitamin D deficiency may be a concern for elderly individuals using sunscreens.
items.reason: Chronologic aging decreases the ability of human skin to produce vitamin D3, and sunscreen use filters out UV-B.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why there is concern about vitamin D deficiency in elderly individuals using sunscreens.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.assertion: Nutritional supplementation is a preferable strategy for addressing vitamin D deficiency.
items.reason: The amount of sunlight needed to produce sufficient vitamin D justifies the risks associated with increased sun exposure.
items.explanation: While nutritional supplementation is preferred, the reason given is false; the small amount of sunlight needed does not justify risks.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 2)

items.assertion: SCCs harbor more oncogenic driver mutations per cell than aged sun-exposed normal skin.
items.reason: SCCs have approximately 10 times more oncogenic driver mutations per cell compared to cells in aged sun-exposed normal skin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why SCCs harbor more mutations.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 3)
items.assertion: MCPyV-positive MCCs grow due to UV-induced mutations.
items.reason: MCPyV-positive tumors grow secondary to viral integration into the host genome.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion as MCPyV-positive growth is linked to viral factors, not UV-induced mutations.

items.reason: The passage states that sunburn is one of the acute effects of sun exposure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 4)
items.assertion: NMSCs are more directly linked to sun exposure than melanoma.
items.reason: The evidence for NMSCs is far more direct compared to melanoma due to their development on sun-exposed areas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why NMSCs have a more direct link to sun exposure.

items.assertion: UVR-induced immunosuppression is linked to an increased risk of nonmelanoma skin cancer in organ transplant recipients.
items.reason: More than 50% of organ transplant recipients develop BCCs and SCCs, which are the most common malignancies in these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: Acute Effects of Sun Exposure The acute effects of skin exposure to sunlight include sunburn and vitamin D synthesis. (part 5)
items.assertion: UV-B activates regulatory T cells that suppress antitumor immune responses.
items.reason: This suppression occurs via IL-10 expression by Langerhans cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not correct because it's UV-B activating regulatory T cells that suppress antitumor immune responses via IL-10, not Langerhans cells.

items.assertion: Phototoxicity requires energy absorption by a drug or chemical.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Polymorphous Light Eruption is more common in men than in women.
items.reason: The condition often manifests with pruritic erythematous papules on exposed areas of the trunk and forearms.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as PMLE is more common in women, but the reason correctly describes a manifestation of PMLE.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 1)

items.assertion: Photoallergy involves an immunopathologic process.

items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.assertion: Patients with chronic actinic dermatitis do not require photoprotection.
items.reason: Chronic actinic dermatitis involves UV hyperresponsiveness and requires meticulous photoprotection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states that these patients require photoprotection.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 2)

items.assertion: Porphyria cutanea tarda can be triggered by increased iron stores.
items.reason: Increased hepatic iron stores are associated with PCT and can reduce uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.assertion: Pseudoporphyria is caused by abnormalities in porphyrin metabolism.
items.reason: Pseudoporphyria resembles PCT clinically but does not involve porphyrin metabolism abnormalities.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pseudoporphyria does not have porphyrin metabolism abnormalities, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 3)

items.assertion: Patients with darker complexion are at decreased risk for acute sunburn and cutaneous malignancy.
items.reason: Individuals of darker complexion have more melanin, which provides natural photoprotection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION

items.assertion: Limiting sun exposure during midday can substantially reduce lifetime UVR exposure.
items.reason: Most of an individual's total lifetime sun exposure occurs by age 18, making education about sunlight hazards crucial.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why limiting midday sun exposure reduces UVR.
items.reference: Photosensitivity and Other Reactions to Sunlight · PHOTOSENSITIVITY DISEASES (part 4) | PHOTOPROTECTION
